svg.aria-label: The pause and resume lifecycle for run run-aa10. The agent runs and journals run_started then the search_policy tool_result. At the next step it hits an approval gate because a 180 dollar refund exceeds the 100 dollar threshold. Instead of calling the tool, interrupt() journals a pending_approval event carrying the token appr-1 and raises a serializable PendingApproval exception. That exception is caught at the TOP LEVEL, not sys.exit, so the process persists the journal, returns the token to the caller, and stays runnable. A gap labelled time passes marks where a human decides later, possibly in a different process. Then resume(run_id, decision) is called with the token: it replays the same persisted journal, memoizing step 0, records an approval_decision event, and continues to execute the refund and finish. The journal is the single artifact that persists across the gap; the token is handed out at the pause and handed back at the resume.
svg.title: Pause and resume: interrupt() raises a serializable PendingApproval (a token) caught at the top level, NOT sys.exit, and resume() replays the persisted journal

svg.text: Run run-aa10. At the gate the agent does not die and does not call the tool: it journals pending_approval, raises PendingApproval, and hands out a token. A human decides later; resume hands the token back.
PROCESS 1: RUN UNTIL THE GATE, THEN PAUSE

svg.text: 3. continue: the gated refund keeps its
idempotency key, so an approved resume
executes EXACTLY ONCE even if retried.

svg.text: The decision is a first-class action, not
just a yes or no: APPROVE, DENY, or STEER
a correction. The three branches are the
A real deployment pauses in one process and resumes in
another. Here both phases run over one persisted journal;
the CLI version is the same log read by a second invocation.
Built on Part 9's journal and idempotency key. The new move is interrupt + resume: a serializable exception (a token),
caught at the top level, lets a durable agent hand control to a human and come back without losing or repeating work.
